title: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the substantive quality of a TIA; surfaces only TIAs that have not been reviewed within policy cadence.
evidence: Saved search 'tia_currency' (weekly) lists TIAs past their review date with the related transfer flows.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.46 SCC safeguards rely on a maintained TIA; detecting staleness is a direct operational control.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.46 (Transfers subject to safeguards) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.38.4: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.4' running on TIA register (tia_register.csv), transfer_register.csv, DPO review events., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: UK equivalent to Art.46 is maintained through TIAs (ICO International Data Transfer Agreement).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK-GDPR Art.46 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.38.4: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.4' running on TIA register (tia_register.csv), transfer_register.csv, DPO review events., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.46 (parent assurance='full'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.46 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.38.4: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.38.4' running on TIA register (tia_register.csv), transfer_register.csv, DPO review events., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A TIA last reviewed 15 months ago (policy cadence: 12 months) appears in the next weekly run.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A TIA reviewed within the last 12 months does not appear.

dataSources: TIA register (tia_register.csv), transfer_register.csv, DPO review events. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| inputlookup tia_register.csv
| eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(last_reviewed,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0)
| where age_days>cadence_days
| lookup transfer_register.csv tia_id OUTPUT transfer_id destination
| table tia_id destination last_reviewed age_days cadence_days transfer_id
description: Makes TIA currency a visible metric rather than a yearly memory item. Stale TIAs are the most common Schrems II finding by supervisory authorities.
value: Converts a calendar reminder into a demonstrable control with evidence per-transfer.
implementation: (1) Maintain tia_register.csv; (2) run weekly; (3) on miss, DPO re-opens the TIA review workflow.
visualization: Table of stale TIAs with remediation owner, bar chart of TIAs by age bucket, single-value 'TIAs within cadence'. Key fields: `age_days`, `cadence_days`, `destination`, `last_reviewed`, `tia_id`, `transfer_id`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `transfer_register.csv`, `tia_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('transfer_register.csv','tia_register.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.46`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.46 (Transfers subject to safeguards) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.38.4: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain tia_register.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** run weekly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** on miss, DPO re-opens the TIA review workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II assessments," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search begins with `| inputlookup tia_register.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `tia_register.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup tia_register.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true tia_register_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.38.4: Transfer Impact Assessment currency — stale Schrems II asses]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.38.4,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `tia_id`, `last_reviewed`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table tia_id, last_reviewed
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `transfer_register.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup transfer_register.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.38.4: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Stale Tias With Remediation Owner (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Tias By Age Bucket (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Tias Within Cadence' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see cross-border data flows against your rules so transfers stay justified and documented.
